metadata.title: Why Your 10x Accelerator Only Made It 3x Faster

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Why Your 10x Accelerator Only Made It 3x Faster

beats.narration_text: Konnichiwa, Kore. This is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. The NPU really is ten times faster than the CPU — and your model only got three times faster.

props.command: My NPU is 10× faster than CPU but my model only ran 3× faster. What happened?
output: The 20% of ops that can't run on the NPU fall back to CPU and dominate total time. Amdahl's law in hardware.

beats.narration_text: Here is why Why Your 10x Accelerator Only Made It 3x Faster belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: My NPU is 10× faster than CPU but my model only ran 3× faster. What happened?
output: The 20% of ops that can't run on the NPU fall back to CPU and dominate total time. Amdahl's law in hardware.

beats.narration_text: From the textbook: a Cortex-M55 host and an Ethos-U55 NPU sharing an SRAM pool. The supported ops route to the NPU. Everything else falls back to the CPU. That handoff is the routing problem.

Watch an op stream routing into your accelerator. Eighty percent of ops — convolutions, matrix multiplies — hit the NPU and fly through. Ten times faster than CPU. Each op: milliseconds. Throughput looks great. The accelerator really works.

The other twenty percent — softmax, reshape, custom activations — aren't supported. They route to CPU. Each one is ten times slower. And they queue. Ops pile up waiting for CPU to clear. That bottleneck is now the whole pipeline. The NPU sits idle while the queue drains.

The math: eighty percent on NPU at ten-times speedup — contributes zero-point-zero-eight of your budget. Twenty percent on CPU at baseline — contributes zero-point-two. Total speedup: one over zero-point-two-eight — about three-point-six times. Not ten. Three-point-six. That's Amdahl's law: the unaccelerated fraction owns your latency ceiling.

Your turn. Ask Claude: My model has open bracket total ops close bracket MACs. My NPU supports open bracket pct NPU close bracket percent of them, runs open bracket npu speed close bracket times faster; the rest fall back to CPU. One — compute total speedup. Two — identify which op types are the fallback bottleneck. Three — what architectural change would raise NPU coverage from open bracket pct NPU close bracket to open bracket pct target close bracket percent. Four — at what NPU coverage does the remaining CPU fallback become negligible.

props.command: My model has [total_ops] MACs. My NPU supports [pct_npu]% of them (runs [npu_speed]× faster); the rest fall back to CPU. (1) Compute total speedup, (2) identify which op types are the fallback bottleneck, (3) what architectural change would raise NPU coverage from [pct_npu]% to [pct_target]%, and (4) at what NPU coverage does the remaining CPU fallback become negligible?
output: Speedup = 1/(npu_frac/npu_speedup + cpu_frac/1). At 80% NPU coverage with 10× speedup: 3.57×. The fallback 20% is the ceiling.
